title: The quiet revolution in edge inference
dek: Why the interesting work in AI this year isn't happening in datacenters.
body:
LEAD: Every press release is about a bigger model. Every interesting engineering story is about a smaller one.

In the last six months, three shifts have compounded: NPUs in every mid-range phone, quantization that actually preserves behavior below 4 bits, and inference runtimes that finally respect memory hierarchy. Individually, each is a footnote. Together they change what an application can assume about its environment.

## What changed, concretely

A year ago, running a competent 7B model on a $200 SBC was a party trick — minutes to first token, RAM pinned, thermal throttling inside of a paragraph. Today it is *routine*, and the bottleneck has moved to storage bandwidth, which is a much more tractable problem.

- Mixed-precision kernels that downshift at runtime under thermal pressure without model reload.
- KV-cache paging over NVMe with prefetch hints from the decoder's own attention pattern.
- Tokenizer fast-paths that skip Python entirely — often the biggest single win.

> The most interesting inference work of 2026 fits on a device you can put in your pocket.

## What to watch

The sleeper trend is *locality-aware routing*: fleets of tiny models that hand queries to each other based on context, with a larger model invoked only as a fallback. The economics are brutal — a $0.02 API call collapses to nearly zero when 80% of it runs on-device.

### My bet

Within eighteen months, the default assumption for a consumer ML feature will be that it runs locally, and calling out to a server will be the exception that demands justification.

title: Latency is the new UX
dek: Below 100ms, interfaces feel like thought. Above it, they feel like websites.
body:
LEAD: There is a line, somewhere around the blink rate, below which software stops feeling like software.

It is the reason local-first apps feel faster than their cloud equivalents even when the cloud one is, on paper, more capable. It is the reason streaming tokens matters more than total throughput. It is why I have mostly given up on apps that put a network round-trip between me and the thing I asked for.

## The 100ms budget

You have roughly a hundred milliseconds to respond to an input before the user's attention starts to audit you. Not finish — just respond. A skeleton, a cursor, a character, anything. This is old news in game dev and new news in LLM-land.

title: I rewrote my market-maker in Rust. Here's what I learned.
dek: Spoiler: the wins weren't where I expected.
body:
LEAD: Every rewrite post has the same arc, and this one is no exception, except for the parts that were surprising.

The conventional wisdom says: rewrite in Rust for performance. In my case the performance gains were real but modest — maybe 1.3× on the hot path. The real wins were elsewhere.

## Borrow checker as type designer

Forced to think about ownership of an order from creation to acknowledgement, I discovered my Python code was implicitly moving orders through at least four owners with no clear handoff. The Rust rewrite made that a compile error. I caught two latent race conditions this way before writing a single test.

title: Vol surface regime-switching with HMMs
dek: A small model that quietly outperforms big ones.
body: The entire field has been chasing transformers on order-book data. Meanwhile a three-state HMM on realized-vol quantiles has been sitting there, posting Sharpe.

title: Sparse attention keeps getting rediscovered
dek: A tour through five papers proposing the same idea with different Greek letters.
body:
LEAD: At this point every sparse-attention paper is a rediscovery. The question is what the rediscovery adds.

This week I read five. Three of them are identical. One of them is clearly better but will be forgotten. One of them is wrong in an interesting way.

title: Five papers I re-read every year
dek: Not the famous ones. The ones that taught me how to think.
body: Most papers are transactional: you read them, extract the finding, move on. A few are different — they reshape how you approach a problem. Here are mine.

title: Hidden gem: lapce
dek: A Rust-native editor that deserves more attention than it gets.
body: Everyone defaults to VS Code and a handful defect to Zed. lapce sits in the corner being quietly excellent.

title: Hidden gem: ast-grep
dek: grep for people who are tired of regex.
body: Structural search for code. It is not new, but it is underused. If you refactor regularly and you are still using regex, you are paying a tax you don't need to.
